pressure_source: Eleven years of fraud confronted by something real — the ritual answers for the first time
state_shift: Maren goes from functioning fraud to genuinely possessed; his System-blindness is suddenly mediated by a dead paladin who despises him
must_land_beats: The fake ritual — show Maren's choreography, the cues he reads from the family, the practiced performance
Something answers — the paladin's consciousness, memories, and combat training hit Maren all at once, physically overwhelming
Maren throws up in the ditch — the supernatural event grounded in the body
Aldric demands prayer; Maren refuses — the power dynamic between them established in the first exchange
Aldric begins reading the System interface with the cadence of a grocery list at a state funeral

setups_to_plant.description: Establish Maren's ceramic eating bowl as an ordinary prop of his fake ritual — no System properties, handled with practiced care out of habit

pressure_source: The impossibility of returning to normal life with a dead paladin narrating everything
state_shift: From shock to uneasy coexistence; Aldric's grief surfaces beneath his sarcasm, complicating the comedy
must_land_beats: Maren's daily fraud routine revealed — the social performance, the cues from others' reactions to System prompts, the small village kindnesses toward the 'blind man'
Aldric's real-time interface reading — the scope of what Maren has been missing for twenty-eight years
Aldric's grief breaking through: he's dead, he had a life, he is trapped
secondary_character_beats: Aldric's grief must register as genuine anguish, not just setup for comedy. He had an order, duties, people who loved him. A moment where the sarcasm drops and Maren doesn't know how to respond.

setups_to_plant.description: Aldric notes something odd about how Maren's consciousness interacts with the System — a quality of absence that doesn't match ordinary blindness
Aldric is oddly specific about certain System details, suggesting pre-existing knowledge of what to look for in Maren's profile

pressure_source: The System has noticed Maren; the bounty makes him visible to every hero in range
state_shift: Maren can no longer be invisible; his relationship with Derry strains under the imported danger
must_land_beats: Derry noticing Maren reacting to System prompts he never acknowledged before
The bounty appearing — impossibly large, visible to every hero
Derry's face when he realizes leaving means splitting from his wife and daughter
The decision to flee Fenhold

pressure_source: An abandoned quest pooling contamination toward a settlement forces Maren to attempt a real consumption he doesn't know how to do
state_shift: Three consciousnesses in one skull; the fragment collision creates an unclassified entity; the paladin's sarcasm fails for the first time
must_land_beats: The abandoned quest-light pooling — Maren choosing to eat it despite not knowing the proper order
The botched consumption — wrong ritual sequence, the assassin fragment (Vess) arriving furious
Vess's immediate fury: about being dead, about the paladin, about the incompetent host
The fragment collision producing something with no System classification
Aldric going quiet — unable or unwilling to describe what he sees

setups_to_plant.description: Vess mentions her unfinished contract and Torren Blackhall in her initial fury — specific enough to register but subordinate to the chaos of the scene

pressure_source: First bounty hunters arrive — trained, System-equipped heroes against a man who has never fought
state_shift: Maren becomes a killer; his desire for authenticity collides with the reality that authenticity begins with blood
must_land_beats: Maren's body fighting without his conscious direction — Aldric's training and Vess's instincts operating while Maren experiences it blind
The disorientation of System-blind combat — no damage numbers, no threat calculations, just sensation and reflex
He kills one hero; the physical specificity of that killing
The aftermath: nausea, shaking hands, horror
Aldric's failed comfort ('a clean kill') and Maren's rage at the framing

pressure_source: The trauma of first killing; the weight of running from a life that was already a lie
state_shift: The emotional baseline is established in human terms — what's at stake is measured by ordinary things
must_land_beats: Maren unable to eat — his body rejecting food after the killing
Derry sharing bread and talking about ordinary things: his daughter, the garden, the cow
Aldric and Vess arguing quietly about next steps (pragmatic vs. moral)
A moment of beauty: quest-light in stream water, Aldric describing it without sarcasm for the first time
secondary_character_beats: Derry's ordinary life must feel specific and valued — his daughter Pell, his wife Lenna's garden, the cow that keeps escaping. These are not color; they are the stakes of his sacrifice.
Aldric and Vess's argument should reveal their incompatible worldviews without either being simply wrong.

setups_to_plant.description: Derry talks about Pell and Lenna with specific, tender detail — the daughter learning to count, the wife's failed herb garden, the ordinariness of what he left behind

pressure_source: A real sin-eater (Thessa) confronts Maren with the specific, countable cost of eleven years of faking
state_shift: Maren's guilt becomes specific and enormous — named villages, countable years, possible deaths; Thessa refuses to help
must_land_beats: Thessa's anger rooted not in dishonesty but in contamination damage — people living in uncleared residue, children growing up in it
Maren failing Thessa's test of basic sin-eating knowledge — comprehensively, embarrassingly
The dead farmer from Chapter 1 potentially being Maren's fault
Thessa's warning about unclassified entities: the System corrects them, the last one left nothing of the host
secondary_character_beats: Thessa's fury must come from specific professional knowledge, not abstract morality. She has seen what contamination does to farmland and children. Her anger is the anger of a woman who has done the real work for decades while frauds collected the same fees.

pressure_source: The fragments are not tools or annoyances but damaged, incomplete people with their own unfinished lives and moral claims
state_shift: The ethical weight of carrying consciousnesses shifts from inconvenience to genuine responsibility; Aldric's death may not have been accidental
must_land_beats: Vess's contract: Torren Blackhall, the massacre, the quest she exploited for access
Vess's incompleteness — memory gaps, a sister whose name is missing, emotions that cut off mid-feeling
Aldric's gaps — he cannot remember how he died, and the omission feels deliberate rather than random
The implication that the System may edit what it absorbs — memories removed, not merely degraded
secondary_character_beats: Vess must register as a person, not a plot device. Her missing sister's name should hurt specifically — she knows the shape of the absence.
Aldric's gaps should frighten him more than death did. He was a man who valued knowledge and order; not knowing is worse than not existing.

pressure_source: A System-blind man with a bounty navigating a town built for System-optimized heroes
state_shift: Maren's cover is blown in a populated area; his empty System profile becomes his most identifiable feature; quiet flight is no longer viable
must_land_beats: The comedy of Harrow — quest boards, gear shops, scorch marks, damage rankings displayed overhead
Aldric narrating frantically; Vess rating the lethality of every hero they pass
Maren's empty System profile drawing attention — a void where a classification should be
Derry trying and failing to act normal
The humor evaporating when a hero recognizes the bounty description
secondary_character_beats: Derry's anxiety should be physical and specific — sweating, over-smiling, talking too much to vendors about things he doesn't need.

pressure_source: Caul's dead party — people who died because contamination was not properly cleared by a sin-eater who was faking
state_shift: Maren cannot hide from the real consequences of his fraud; the threat from Caul is moral, not physical; Derry's trust visibly erodes
must_land_beats: Caul choosing to talk rather than attack — worse for Maren than a fight would be
Caul's specific loss: Haley and Renn, three months ago, a dungeon that should have been clean
Maren recognizing the pattern of his own fraud in Caul's story without being able to confess
Derry's face during the conversation — he knows what Maren is hearing
Caul's day's head start: not mercy but procedure, wanting Maren to reach the Crook for proper judgment
secondary_character_beats: Caul's grief must register as specific loss, not generic righteousness. He lost named people. His pursuit is mourning in the shape of duty.
Derry's trust erosion should be visible in body language and small choices — where he sits, whether he meets Maren's eyes.

payoffs_to_land.description: Derry's investment in his family (established Ch 6) now visibly conflicts with his loyalty to Maren — the cost of continued companionship is measured against what he left behind

pressure_source: The gap between fraudulent performance and genuine practice — Maren must open himself to the contamination in a way that requires sincerity he has never practiced
state_shift: Maren begins genuine sin-eating practice driven by the need to stop being a fraud; he is terrible at it but trying; Aldric observes something new about the System
must_land_beats: Maren choosing to practice on his own — using fragments of Thessa's teaching, Aldric's theoretical knowledge, Vess's pragmatism
The physical reality of real consumption — opening himself to contamination, the body's rejection
Half-success: some residue cleared but at physical cost — hands and knees, nausea, tremors
Aldric going quiet again during the consumption — seeing something in the System's architecture
Vess's unexpected gentleness during recovery

pressure_source: What the paladin has been seeing from inside a System-blind host — the System's architecture, its true nature, and what Maren represents to it
state_shift: The central mystery cracks open: System-blindness is immunity, the System is containment, Maren is a crack in the wall; Aldric admits he chose Maren
must_land_beats: Aldric describing the System's architecture: not interface but containment structure with edges, walls, borders
System-blindness recontextualized: not deficiency but what happens when the System cannot write on you
The unclassified entity as a widening gap in the wall the System has maintained
Aldric admitting he reached for Maren deliberately — he was investigating System anomalies before his death and needed a System-blind host
The implication that Aldric's dungeon-collapse death may have been a System correction

payoffs_to_land.description: The odd quality Aldric noted in Chapter 2 resolves: Maren's consciousness doesn't interact with the System because the System cannot write on it — immunity, not absence
Aldric's oddly specific knowledge resolves: he reached for Maren deliberately, seeking a System-blind host for his investigation

pressure_source: Derry's parenthood versus his loyalty — the weight of a daughter waiting at home against the danger of staying with Maren
state_shift: Maren loses his oldest human connection; Derry leaves; the last tie to Maren's pre-possession identity is severed; Caul knows their direction
must_land_beats: The accumulating small betrayals of warmth: shorter answers, meals facing the road, jokes that don't land
Maren seeing the withdrawal clearly (he reads people professionally) but refusing to name it
Derry's departure: quiet, specific, understandable — Pell needs her father
The betrayal is not dramatic; it is a man choosing his child over his friend
secondary_character_beats: Derry's departure must be driven by love for his daughter, not cowardice. He is not a villain. He is a parent making an impossible calculation that has only one answer.

payoffs_to_land.description: Everything Derry described in Chapter 6 — Pell learning to count, Lenna's garden, the ordinary life — is the reason he leaves. The specific tenderness of those details becomes the weight that breaks the friendship.

pressure_source: The sin-eater community examines Maren and finds him dangerous; Thessa argues for consuming him; he must demonstrate control he doesn't fully have
state_shift: The community that should be his home nearly destroys him; he survives through accidental utility, not competence; Thessa begins grudgingly working with him
must_land_beats: The Crook — smaller than expected, a handful of sin-eaters doing essential thankless work in contamination-heavy territory
The examination: fragments not sitting right, unclassified entity growing, contamination being redistributed rather than cleared
Thessa arguing for consumption — breaking Maren down the way quests are broken down
The supervised consumption: barely managed, but contamination decreases across a wider area than any individual sin-eater can affect
Thessa's acknowledgment: 'not entirely useless'
secondary_character_beats: Thessa's private doubt should surface: the real sin-eating is getting harder, contamination is getting denser, and she has no one to tell. Maren's wider-area capability challenges her understanding of what sin-eating can be.

pressure_source: Caul arrives with a full hero party at the Crook; sin-eaters die; the System responds directly with a local reclassification
state_shift: The System stops using intermediaries and acts directly; sin-eaters die because of Maren; Caul witnesses the System's true nature and his certainty fractures
must_land_beats: Sin-eaters dying — people with nothing to do with Maren, doing their jobs, killed because of his presence
Maren eating a quest mid-combat to disrupt hero targeting — real sin-eating used tactically
The consumption overload: the quest is too large, the unclassified entity surges
The System's immune response: local reclassification, contamination spike, threat creatures spawning
Caul stopping his pursuit of Maren to fight what the System is sending — his faith fracturing
secondary_character_beats: Caul's faith in the System must break in a specific moment — seeing the System sacrifice its own loyal heroes to maintain classification. His entire justification for hunting Maren was that the System was trustworthy.
Thessa in combat should show decades of competence — she's not a fighter but she knows contamination and can manipulate it defensively.

pressure_source: Vess's continued presence destabilizes the unclassified entity; she chooses to leave knowing it means dissipation
state_shift: One of the three is gone; Maren is diminished; the unclassified entity stabilizes but the skull is quieter; the ceramic bowl performs real work for the first time
must_land_beats: Vess identifying herself as the destabilizing factor — two incompatible fragments are worse than one
Vess's choice: a slow fade over continued existence in Maren's skull
The Blackhall contract released — it matters less than she thought, or it matters but not more than this
The separation ritual using Maren's ceramic bowl — the fake tool doing real work
Vess leaving the way a sound leaves a room
Aldric's silence after Vess is gone
secondary_character_beats: Aldric's silence must carry the weight of an adversary's absence. He argued with Vess constantly; her leaving removes a structural element of his existence inside Maren. The silence is not relief.

payoffs_to_land.description: Vess releases the Blackhall contract — her unfinished business resolved not through completion but through the realization that presence matters more than vengeance
Maren's ceramic bowl — introduced in Chapter 1 as a prop of his fake ritual — performs genuine sin-eating work for the first time during the separation ritual

pressure_source: The Woundmouth — the thinnest point in the System's architecture — where Maren must consume the System's wound itself to understand what he is
state_shift: The mystery resolves: the System is desperate containment, Maren is both the crack and the only one who can seal it; he chooses maintenance over destruction
must_land_beats: The Woundmouth: a dungeon mouth where quest-light bleeds rather than pools, contamination so old it has become structural
Maren seeing the System interface for the first and only time — light, numbers, framework — and behind it, the machinery
The System as desperate containment: not malevolent, not benevolent, but holding something wilder and older in check
Maren as both crack and sealant — the only one who can touch both sides because the System cannot write on him
The consumption nearly killing him; Aldric holding him together through stubbornness, not heroism
Maren choosing to maintain the wall rather than break it — a practical decision, not a heroic one

payoffs_to_land.description: The full revelation of the System's nature, seeded through Aldric's observations and the hero-town architecture: it is a containment structure, and what it contains is the unclassifiable world that preceded it

setups_to_plant.description: The hero-town infrastructure in Chapter 9 and Aldric's architectural description in Chapter 12 resolve here as the full picture of the System's purpose

pressure_source: The question of what Maren does now that he understands what he is and has chosen the work
state_shift: Maren is no longer a fraud; he chose the work knowing its cost; the novel closes with him doing the job — still blind, still accompanied, still ordinary
must_land_beats: Thessa acknowledging Maren as a colleague — professional recognition, not warmth
The question of Derry left open — Maren understands the betrayal without forgiving it
Aldric still reading the interface, still sarcastic, but with something gentler underneath
Maren performing a real sin-eating ritual — properly, with understanding, seeing nothing
The closing echo: something answered, and he answered back
secondary_character_beats: Thessa's acknowledgment should be minimal and professional — she tells him the next contamination site. That's it. That's enough.
Aldric's changed tone should be subtle — a slightly different cadence, a description offered without being asked, a joke that's gentle instead of cutting.
